title: List Comprehensions
subtitle: Write concise, readable lists in a single line
learning_objectives: Explain the list comprehension syntax [expression for item in iterable]
Write a list comprehension that applies an expression to each item
Add an if condition to filter items in a comprehension
Compare a list comprehension to its equivalent for loop
Decide when a comprehension improves readability vs when a for loop is clearer

sections.heading: The Basic List Comprehension
sections.content:
A list comprehension is a compact way to build a list from an iterable. Instead of writing a for loop that calls .append(), you write the whole thing inside square brackets in one line.

The syntax is: [expression for item in iterable]

Python evaluates expression for every item in the iterable and collects the results into a new list.
sections.code_example:
# Traditional for-loop approach
squares_loop = []
for x in range(5):
    squares_loop.append(x ** 2)
print(squares_loop)

# Equivalent list comprehension
squares = [x ** 2 for x in range(5)]
print(squares)

# Works with any iterable
words = ["hello", "world", "python"]
upper = [w.upper() for w in words]
print(upper)

sections.heading: Adding a Condition with if
sections.content: You can add an if clause to the end of a comprehension to filter which items get included. Only items for which the condition is True will produce a value in the output list.
sections.code_example:
# All even numbers from 0 to 9
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)

# Words longer than 4 characters
words = ["hi", "hello", "hey", "howdy", "yo"]
long_words = [w for w in words if len(w) > 4]
print(long_words)

# Combine expression and filter
even_squares = [x ** 2 for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(even_squares)

sections.heading: When to Use Comprehensions vs For Loops
sections.content:
List comprehensions shine when the logic fits on one readable line. They can be faster than equivalent for loops because they are optimised internally by Python.

Use a comprehension when: the expression is simple and the intent is obvious at a glance.

Use a for loop when: you need multiple statements inside the body, nested logic would make the comprehension hard to read, or you need to do something other than build a list (like print each item).

Readability always wins. A comprehension that needs a comment to understand is a for loop in disguise.
sections.code_example:
# Good use of comprehension — clear intent
lengths = [len(w) for w in ["cat", "elephant", "ox"]]
print(lengths)

# For loop is better here — multiple steps per item
results = []
for num in range(5):
    if num != 0:
        results.append(100 // num)
print(results)
sections.note: Nested list comprehensions (a comprehension inside a comprehension) are possible but quickly become unreadable. Prefer nested for loops when there are two levels of iteration.

exercises.title: Squares List
exercises.prompt: Use a list comprehension to create a variable called squares that contains the squares of numbers 1 through 8 (inclusive). Print the list.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a variable called squares using a list comprehension
# It should contain the squares of numbers 1 through 8
# Print squares
exercises.solution:
squares = [x ** 2 for x in range(1, 9)]
print(squares)
hints: Use range(1, 9) to get numbers 1 through 8.
The expression for each item is x ** 2.

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.

exercises.title: Filter Odd Numbers
exercises.prompt: Use a list comprehension with an if condition to create a variable called odds that contains only the odd numbers from 1 to 15 (inclusive). Print the list.
exercises.starter_code:
# Create a variable called odds using a list comprehension with a condition
# It should contain only odd numbers from 1 to 15
# Print odds
exercises.solution:
odds = [x for x in range(1, 16) if x % 2 != 0]
print(odds)
hints: The condition for odd numbers is: x % 2 != 0

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.

exercises.title: Upper-Case Short Words
exercises.prompt: Given the list animals = ["cat", "elephant", "ox", "dog", "hippopotamus"], use a list comprehension to create a variable called short_upper containing the upper-cased versions of animals whose names are 3 characters or fewer. Print the result.
exercises.starter_code:
animals = ["cat", "elephant", "ox", "dog", "hippopotamus"]
# Create a variable called short_upper using a list comprehension
# Include only animals with names 3 characters or fewer, converted to uppercase
# Print short_upper
exercises.solution:
animals = ["cat", "elephant", "ox", "dog", "hippopotamus"]
short_upper = [a.upper() for a in animals if len(a) <= 3]
print(short_upper)
hints: The condition filters words with len(a) <= 3.
The expression transforms each kept word with .upper().

tests.message: Your code should run without errors.

quiz.question: What does [x * 2 for x in range(4)] produce?
quiz.explanation: range(4) produces 0, 1, 2, 3. Multiplying each by 2 gives 0, 2, 4, 6.

quiz.question: In [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0], what does the if clause do?
options: It raises an error if x is odd
It runs a separate loop for even numbers only
It filters the iterable so only even values of x are included in the result
quiz.explanation: The if clause is a filter. Items for which the condition is False are simply skipped and do not appear in the output list.

completion_message: Well done! List comprehensions are one of Python's most loved features. With a filter condition and an expression you can replace many multi-line loops with a single clear line.
